title: Macramé fruit bowl
subtitle: A freestanding knotted cord bowl, approximately 25 cm diameter, in natural cotton macramé cord
excerpt: A macramé fruit bowl is built from a knotted base worked outward from a central ring, with the sides shaped by gathering and square-knotting the cords upward. The finished bowl is firm enough to hold fruit and light enough to sit on a kitchen counter or table. This entry uses 5 mm natural cotton cord.
sourceNotes: Macramé fruit bowl construction synthesised from general macramé bowl knotting practice. The radial base built from a central ring is a standard approach for three-dimensional macramé vessels.

glossaryTerms.definition: The core macramé knot, made by crossing the left working cord over two filler cords and through the right loop, then the right working cord over and through the left loop. Two square knots tied in sequence produce a flat, balanced unit.
The folded loop mounting knot used to attach cord lengths to a ring or dowel. One cord folded in half forms two working ends when larks-headed onto a support.
A single loop of the working cord around the filler cord, used to create diagonal ribbing or to lock a finished row in place.
A wrapping knot that binds multiple cords together at a single point, used to pull the sides of the bowl into shape and divide cord groups into working sets.

content.text: A macramé fruit bowl is shaped by knotting radially outward from a central metal ring to form the base, then gathering the cords upward and knotting the sides. The
is the only structural knot used: the entire bowl is a variation of single and grouped square knots. The shape is controlled by the frequency and placement of
that pull groups of cords together as the sides build up. Use 5 mm natural cotton cord: thinner cord makes the bowl too floppy; thicker cord makes the knots unwieldy at this scale.

items.name: Metal ring, 10 cm diameter (the bowl base ring)
Metal ring, 25 cm diameter (the bowl rim ring)

content.text: Step 1: Mount the cords on the base ring
Cut 16 lengths of cord at 220 cm each. Fold each length in half and attach to the small base ring using a
knot, spacing them evenly around the ring. You now have 32 working ends. Pin the base ring to the macramé board and work radially outward from the ring.

Work in groups of 4 cords (2 working, 2 filler). Tie 3 square knots per group, approximately 2-3 cm from the base ring. This first round produces the inner base. Re-group the cords: take 2 ends from the right of one group and 2 ends from the left of the adjacent group to form 16 new groups of 4. Tie 2 square knots per new group, 2 cm below the first round. Continue with alternating square knot rounds until the base measures 22-24 cm across.

Bring all 32 cords together and thread them through the large rim ring. Hold the ring above the knotted base at the desired bowl height (approximately 12-15 cm). Pull the cords through the ring evenly, spacing them around the circumference. Tie a single
from each cord over the rim ring to lock its position, then re-group into groups of 4 and tie 2 square knots below the ring to stabilise the rim.

Divide the cords below the rim into 4 groups of 8. Tie a gathering knot around each group approximately 3 cm below the rim ring. Take a separate 30 cm cord, wrap it tightly around the group 8-10 times, then thread the end back through the wraps and pull firmly. Trim the tails to 5-6 cm and unravel the cut ends into a short fringe.

items.problem: The base is floppy and does not hold its shape
items.cause: The cord is too thin for the scale of the bowl or the square knots are too loose
items.fix: Pull each square knot firmly to seat it before moving to the next. For a firmer bowl, stiffen the inside of the base by spraying with a light starch solution and allowing to dry flat.

items.problem: The bowl sides do not sit upright: they lean outward
items.cause: The base is too wide relative to the height of the sides, or the cords were not pulled through the rim ring at equal tension
items.fix: Pull the cords through the rim ring more firmly before tying the half-hitches. The rim ring must be snug against the gathered cords to hold the bowl sides upright.

items.problem: The cord groups are uneven in the base: some sections have more cord than others
items.cause: The lark's head knots were not spaced evenly around the base ring
items.fix: Spacing matters most in the first mounting step. Pin each lark's head knot in position with a T-pin as you attach it, measuring the gap between knots before tying each one.
